questions.questionText: A chronic alcoholic presents with confusion, ophthalmoplegia, and ataxia. This triad is diagnostic of:
questions.optionB: Wernicke encephalopathy (acute thiamine (B1) deficiency — triad: confusion, ophthalmoplegia, ataxia — medical emergency, treat with IV thiamine BEFORE glucose)
questions.explanation: Core concept: Wernicke's triad = Confusion + Ophthalmoplegia + Ataxia (thiamine deficiency). Why correct: Wernicke encephalopathy: acute, reversible (if treated). Thiamine deficiency → hemorrhagic lesions in mammillary bodies, thalamus, periaqueductal gray. Complete triad present in only ~33% — low threshold to treat. Why A wrong: Korsakoff psychosis: CHRONIC sequela of Wernicke's — anterograde/retrograde amnesia + confabulation. Wernicke-Korsakoff syndrome = Wernicke → Korsakoff if untreated. Why C wrong: DT: 48-72h after last drink, severe withdrawal — tremors, hallucinations (visual), seizures, autonomic instability. Treat with benzodiazepines. Why D wrong: Hepatic encephalopathy: asterixis, altered consciousness, liver failure. Clinical relevance: ALWAYS give thiamine BEFORE glucose in alcoholics (glucose metabolism depletes thiamine further → can precipitate Wernicke's). Exam trap: Give thiamine FIRST, glucose SECOND — commonly tested point. Memory aid: 'Wernicke = We Three (confusion, ophthalmoplegia, ataxia) + thiamine saves.' Reference: Ahuja – Substance Use.

questions.questionText: The timeline for onset of alcohol withdrawal seizures after last drink is:
questions.optionB: 24-48 hours (generalized tonic-clonic seizures — 'rum fits'; if seizures occur >48 hours, consider other causes)
questions.explanation: Core concept: Alcohol withdrawal timeline is a HIGH-YIELD exam topic. Why correct: Timeline after last drink: 6-24h = tremors, anxiety, insomnia, GI symptoms. 12-48h = alcoholic hallucinosis (visual/tactile — formication). 24-48h = withdrawal seizures ('rum fits'). 48-96h (peak 72h) = delirium tremens (DT) — most dangerous (5-10% mortality). Why A wrong: Within 6h = early withdrawal symptoms (mild tremor). Why C wrong: 7-10 days — too late. Why D wrong: Symptoms don't start immediately (alcohol still present). Clinical relevance: Treatment: benzodiazepines (chlordiazepoxide for mild, lorazepam/diazepam for severe). CIWA score monitors severity. Seizure prophylaxis: not routine (benzos cover). Exam trap: Hallucinations in alcohol withdrawal occur BEFORE DT. DT is the LAST and MOST SEVERE manifestation. Memory aid: 'Alcohol withdrawal = 6-12-24-48-72 hours: Tremors→Hallucinations→Seizures→DT.' Reference: Ahuja – Alcohol.
